transcript: Most people looking into the E-2 visa end up stuck in the same question. Should I buy an existing business, start my own, or go with a franchise? If that's where you are right now, you're not alone. This is exactly what hundreds of clients we assist every year go through. You're thinking about a big move to the United States, maybe relocating for a few years or indefinitely, creating something for you and your family, building a new life down here. The E-2 visa seems like a great path to get there. But then you hit this decision point. You don't know what business to choose. So, what do people do? They hit Google. They go on BizBuySell. They start browsing listings. They look at that dream business for sale just for 80K. They start thinking, maybe I just go buy something down there. Or they think, why don't I just start my own business? I always, you know, loved I love coffee. I'm just going to open a coffee shop. Or they start entertaining the idea of a franchise. And all of that is completely normal. They start looking into these different options and weigh their pros and cons. Let's start with franchises. This is the closest thing you have to a controlled structure path. You're not guessing, you're stepping into a proven model with a known brand and a system that tells you exactly what to do from day one, and as well as on ongoing basis. From an immigration perspective, that's incredibly powerful because the officer reviewing your case is going to be asking one main question. Do I trust this person to execute the business plan? A franchise answers that question for you. You're signing a contract for 10 years. You have the support from the franchisor. You're committed. You're not relying just on some idea, you're relying on a system that already works. The average age of franchise brands that we work with at Visa Franchise, it's 30-plus years. So, who is this best for? If you've never run a business before, if you're coming from a corporate or employment background, or if you simply want something predictable and structured, this is usually your strongest option. And here's the part most people don't fully appreciate. You're not just paying franchise fees, you're paying for reduction in the execution risk. And in immigration, lower execution risk means higher approval probability. Here's the catch, not every franchise is suited for the E-2 visa. Either franchisors don't want to work with E-2 visa applicants, or they do not have adequate support packages for you. Here at Visa Franchise, we've created franchises that not only are willing to work with our clients, but also have the support systems in place to assist them throughout the visa process and their transition into the United States. Now, let's talk about starting your own business. This is where things get a little more emotional because a lot of people think, "I want to build something from scratch." And there's nothing wrong with that, but you've got to ask the key question. Do you actually know what you're doing in that space? Because starting a business is already difficult. Now, you got to add in that it's a different country, a new market, a different legal system. You got the pressures of the visa, and it becomes even harder. So, when does this option actually make sense? It works when you're not really starting from from zero, starting from scratch. It works when you've already doing business successfully in your home country, and you're simply expanding or replicating it in the United States. Say you're a Canadian equipment company already selling to Americans, or you provide professional services already to Americans, a Canadian software company and you already sell to Americans, or you're a marketing agency. You have one American company in Canada and you think you could also handle their US affairs. That makes sense because you're transferring experience. You're you're not experimenting. You've you've already tested the market a little bit. But if you're still figuring it out, trying ideas, testing things, your odds of success drop quickly because uncertainty in business translates directly into risk in immigration. For our clients who own businesses in their home countries, we've created a solution just for you. A to Z, we help bring your company to the United States, help it get the administrative visas sorted so you can scale up. Registering your company LLC, getting your EIN tax number, opening your business bank account, preparing your business plan. Even in certain cases, we ask our clients to engage local CPA to get their intellectual property, inventory, equipment valued and transfer it into the new US entity to form a portion of their investment for US immigration purposes. This then allows them to commit less cash as they've already invested a substantial amount of value into that US entity. Now, let's talk about buying an existing business. This is the one people initially feel the most comfortable with and ironically, it's often the riskiest because on the surface, it looks simple. There's already revenue, there are already customers. It feels like you're stepping into something stable. But what you're actually buying is someone else's structure, their problems, maybe some of their client accounts, and someone's else has a reason for for leaving and selling that business. A lot of small business owners sell because they burn out, the business is a client, it's really hard to operate, they're working countless hours. So, unless you really understand that industry and you have a clear path to improve the business, this becomes a very risky move. The biggest mistake people make here is thinking, "I'll just take over and keep running things." That rarely works. Revenue drops 10, 15%, profit drops even more because the moment the original owner leaves, the dynamic of the business changes. Those employee relationships, vendor relationships, client relationships, and if you don't know how to manage that, things can fall apart quickly. And add to that all the other things you're juggling, immigration process, relocation, putting your kids in the new school, renting a home, getting insurance, buying a car, etc., that pressure can be overwhelming with a failing business. So, if we simplify everything, franchises tend to give you the highest probability of approval and a structured path towards making money as they reduce execution risk. Starting your own business can give you the freedom in decision-making and structuring it however you like. It can be a great option only if you already know the business deeply. And buying an existing business only works if you treat it as something you need to actually actively improve and not something you can take over and and maintain. So, let me be direct to you. Most people don't fail it cuz they had bad intentions. They failed because they made the wrong business decision early and didn't realize it until it was too late. After they invested, after they committed, after they lost flexibility, that's exactly what we help prevent. We help you choose the right business model, structure it properly, and build a case that actually works. So, if you're serious about doing this right the first time, go to visafranchise.com. Take our eligibility test. And if we believe we can help you, we'll reach out to schedule a discovery solution call. We'll look at your situation, tell you exactly which path makes sense for you, and just as importantly, if it doesn't make sense, we'll tell you that, too. Until next time, thanks for watching.

I keep hearing this all the time. There are families, they either have family members, you know, maybe they have the applicant's mother, right? And they want to bring their mother here, and the mother has been a part of their business or what they've been doing, and they want to make sure the mom is a part of the um the new business, or they would have children who are over 21, maybe 22 years old, maybe 23 years old. And a father is the original applicant, and um they want to make sure that the daughter joins them as well, because the spouse gets a dependent visa. In such cases where family members, immediate family members, going 50/50 in an E-2 business. First of all, what do you think about that? Second of all, would a father gifting money to the daughter to be invested into the E-2 business, which you which is then going to give the daughter 50% of the funds, which is going to qualify them as um an independent applicant? What sort of issues around that do you see, and what's a good strategy to sort of like make sure that's that's consistent? >> Yeah, I see you're right. This is something we see a lot of. Um and it touches a little bit on what my job is, right? Why I'm here to begin with. Um and sometimes I'm I become the least favorite person of of my clients, because I bring problems, I bring negativity, I bring criticism. And and this issue is one where I bring a lot of that stuff. You don't need someone to pat you on the back. You don't need someone to tell you your plan is brilliant, everything's going to work out, right? You need someone to be critical. And if a client walked in the door and said, "Listen, here's the plan. Uh me and my daughter both want an E-2 visa. My daughter's 24 years old, doesn't have a lot of money yet, but I've got some money. So, I'm going to gift her a certain amount of money and she's going to use that to apply and she'll own 50% of the business. On the other hand, I will invest my money into the other 50% of the business and we will both apply for the E-2 investor visa." On paper, that's doable. So long as each applicant has at least 50% control of that company in the United States, they both can credibly present themselves as E-2 investor applicants. But if I put on my critical glasses and I pretend I'm the US government for a while, right? I'm going to start asking some hard questions. What does your daughter do? Is she just a front? We know you love her. Everyone loves their daughter, we hope. We know you guys want to live together in the United States. That's all well and good. How is she really an investor owner? She's only 24 years old. What kind of experience does she have? The money's really not even hers. It comes from you, doesn't it look a little bit like you're just looping her in? She's kind of a front, right? So, there's good answers to all of these objections and there's ways to do it, but it's important you start with those hard questions. Um and those would be some of my questions in the circumstances you're describing. >> Yeah. And if you were to get around that, I don't know if get getting around is the right word, but if you were to sort of like put together a viable petition, what would you do? How would you build that petition? >> I would focus really intensely on the daughter as the applicant, right? Um presuming she's young. Why does she have the skills and the experience necessary to run a business? Well, let's really fortify that. Let's really underscore she's got really good education. She has some professional experience. Uh she has the right kind of resume to run this kind of business, right? Really emphasize that despite her youth, she will make a good business owner. Um and we've had plenty of clients over the last couple months who are in their mid-20s applying with E-2s and have been successful. But you have to overcome that prejudice um of isn't this person too young to run a business? And then you add to that the second question of this person doesn't seem to be really using their own money, right? Um and so I would really underscore that the gift of those funds is truly freely given. That there is a time this daughter has that money and she can do anything she wants with it. She can keep it in Canada, open a business there. She can keep it in Spain. She can go to Las Vegas and gamble at a casino. It's her money. Um it's her investment because truly it was freely given to her. Where it becomes problematic is if there's the perception that the money is just passing through her. It's the dad's money all along passing through the daughter back into the business that the dad's going to own half of. That's a danger point. So, I think the two points of emphasis is are are are on why the daughter, what makes her a good business owner, and that this money was really given to her to do anything she wants with this money. >> Yeah, I mean another aspect to look into this is sometimes parents could be, you know, at a certain age, you know, over 60, 65, whatever the case may be, and every investment to the US is a long-term investment. And they may or may not see themselves running that business for an extended period of time, but they don't want that, you know, 4-year, 5-year of grinding to go to waste. And that's why they they want to prep up a family member to sort of like get in there, grind with them, and then later on take over that business. That's a valid argument as well. And it also always helps, and based on my understanding, and correct me if I'm wrong, is if the family member has, whether that's a daughter or the mom or or or whatever the case may be, if they have their own money, their own funds, then that makes everything ever so easier. Am I correct on that assumption, Grant? >> 100% 100%. Yeah. It it ties back a little bit to that question from the beginning of our conversation. Why you and why not an American? Right? The government's going to say, "Our preference is always that it's an American citizen. You want to come in and run the business for 5 years and then retire and in the meantime train somebody up to take over? We want you to do that with an American. Why your daughter, right?" And the answer to why your daughter is the same answer there there was for why you. And that's because your daughter is assuming risk. And she should be rewarded for that risk. What type of risk? Financial risk. She's putting her money at risk and asking for the visa in exchange. So, anytime there's an applicant, that's always the dynamic. The answer to why you is because I'm putting my money at risk. >> One last question on the um immediate family partnerships. Sometimes clients want to bring over their immediate family members, whether their mothers, daughters, sons, as their essential employee. I know you you're going to say that but building that particular that organic relationship of why this person is essential as opposed to an American apart from that person being your immediate family member um needs convincing amount of documentation and evidence. But suppose that there is no apparent let's say that relationship apart from them having run a different business together at one point or working in the same industries or whatever the case may be. First of all, what do you think about the essential employee route for family members? And at the very least the bare minimum requirements of how that relationship apart from the family relationship should look like for a successful E-2 essential employee visa for a family member. >> Sure. So what you're talking about here is the kind of sister visa to the E-2 investor. And as people probably know, it's called the E-2 essential employee visa. And the E-2 essential employee visa is premised on the business itself being determined an E-2 business. So that investment has to be there. The US government has to recognize the investment and register the business as an E-2 business. When that business is understood and registered as an E-2 business, someone who is not the investor can apply for an E-2 visa by arguing my work as an employee of this business is essential to its survival. And if the government agrees, that person will receive an E-2 visa with all the same benefits as the E-2 investor. It will last just as long as an investor's visa will last. It's renewable indefinitely. It permits the person to bring their spouse with work authorization. It permits the person to bring their minor children, they can attend school. So, it's got a lot of benefits, but it's all hinging on that argument that my work in this company is essential. And you really have to prove that. Um in your hypothetical Aladdin, this person is a family member to the E-2 investor. That will raise the level of skepticism, in my opinion. It will cause the government to say, "We really want to make sure you're essential." Because we know, we're humans, we have families as well, that your dad probably just wants you to live nearby. Or you would just want your mother to live nearby. So, it's not enough that you guys love each other. This is premised on that your work is essential. And they mean it when they say essential. Without you, this business won't survive. Right? How do you demonstrate that? You show your resume. You show your skills. You show your involvement with the planning of the business. You show the relationships you might have with clientele or customers. You really build yourself up as an essential worker. And it's fine that you're family, there's no rules against it, and they won't even admit, I think, that they look at family members harder, but I suspect they do.

Do it. >> Do it. >> Uh, if you decide and if you want to be in the US and you want to be part of the US, be confident and trust yourself. >> Ask for help. >> Ask for help, then stay on the path and don't stop. Look back, but don't go back. Um, it's a beautiful area of Europe, if not the world. And I'm a classic car guy and I have a workshop and I love the car culture in the USA and so, we fell in love. >> And I love the beach. >> You know I love that in Germany. I guess the lakes, but >> Yeah. >> So, was the nature, the vastness of the country? You don't have that in Europe, you know, I know I've spent a lot of time in France, where my wife's from and you drive, no matter where in France, every 5 or 10 minutes there's a village. Where the American West, you could drive for an hour, 2 hours and there there's nothing. It's just open. >> Yeah, that's also a good uh, thing for us to ride off the country and yeah, the the long distances and you feel like you have more space and the freedom is a little bit with you. So on this long distances and yeah, we like that. >> And Martin, remind me when we first spoke, was it probably 18 months ago, two almost two years ago, you had you were working, I think you had a your own business in the classic cars. It was a side project. Remind me how you how you went about deciding that this was going to be your way into the US and in your main focus. >> Yes, so we established a classic car and bike workshop in Germany and we did some restorations and customer repair and car care for them. And was growing to a storage with fully [snorts] service for transport, taking care of paperwork and all the things you have to deal with when you have collect their collector cars. So and when we were here the energy or the culture and community with classic cars and bikes is high vibrant here and we have the service in Germany or like one hand for collector cars and bikes and we thought this service could be here growing as well really good and so we decide to start with the service around the classic cars and services and care and storage and buying selling paperwork registration all of that. So and we >> So one-stop shop essentially for classic car aficionados and and owners. >> Yes, so that's a really mostly service work so what we offer. Yes. >> So tell me about your ideal client. Who do you want to be serving? >> So we serving for example people with high-value collector cars and classic bikes and we establishing the trust between the buyer and the seller. We can organize the transport and logistics and paperwork to have this service completely done with all regulations and all over the US from state to state and it's a like no-brainer complete service when you need to sell or buy a very special limited edition car or bike. >> And we're talking about European made, American made, Japanese made or or doesn't or it's pretty flexible in that regard? >> Yeah, we also offer that when you need to be in contact with European sellers or buyers. We can set up the trustful base for the paperwork, the transport, the payment, whatever is bind to that. we handle all around these ways from door to door uh from customer to customer no matter where you are, even in Italy or France or Germany when you find a car there or you find a car in the US or need to have it in Europe. We have, for example, motorcycles from the World War II and they don't have a really high value because it's a little motorcycle. It's about 10,000 to maximum I would say uh 30,000. And with a car, it can go up to a half million. So, and if you need to have that safe we provide that. Like I've come across people that have 20 20 cars in the United States. And I'm sure some of those are are are classic vehicles. >> That's correct. That's awesome. And remind me, how did you discover the E-2 investor visa? >> Yes, so we went here for the third time, I think, and that was the point when we decide, okay, we need more time to establish our company and to be part of the car culture, we have to be here and and represent and offer our yeah, our passion and and share it with the people and go into the culture as well. So. >> And for for the E-2, did you look at other other ways to move to the United States, other visa categories, or was it pretty early on that you determined the E-2 investor visa was the best path? >> We don't have a clue what opportunities there open for us, so and then we compared all the visa opportunities and after a while we >> Get lost in our mind. >> Yeah. >> too much. >> You have A to Z visa categories. >> A A to Z and we couldn't find the exactly fitting visa category with all the regulations with our business private or what else they've got with which sports and arts and special visas, so we decide to get help from the agency and we saw your services and so we decide to get >> help. >> Ask for help and yes, so that's the way we get connected to you and the Visa Franchise and started the process in >> And then it was um I think a short decision to think about the E2 because of the workshop at home. >> Yeah. >> And then we started this way. >> The circumstances for us was fitting to the E2 visa. But we checked that by our own, and there are so many um different categories and regulations, so we decide to >> ask for help. >> Ask ask you to do the the E2 or the visa, what is that, comparing and set up perfectly for us. Why you want to be there?
